beats.prompt: Match each streak to the principle it illustrates.

pairs.right: gambler's fallacy — no tally is kept

feedback.correct: Memorylessness travels: the coin ignored its run, and so does the weather — the next state reads only 'now', never the streak.
hints: You met this with the coin — a long run of heads doesn't bend the next flip.
A Markov process keeps no tally of its run. P(rain tomorrow | rain today) = 7/10 whether it's rained one day or ten — the streak length is not part of the state. 'Due' is the gambler's fallacy.
Match each run to 'only now matters': 9 heads → still 1/2; 9 wet days → still 7/10; 'we're due' → gambler's fallacy.

beats.prompt: It has rained nine days straight. A friend says 'we're due for clear skies.' What happens to tomorrow's rain probability?

options: Lower — nine wet days, it's due to clear
The same 7/10 — only 'today is wet' matters
Higher — wet weather feeds on itself

Lower — nine wet days, it's due to clear.note: That's the gambler's fallacy. A Markov process keeps no tally of its run — P(rain tomorrow | rain today) = 7/10 whether it rained one day or ten. The streak length is not part of the state.

The same 7/10 — only 'today is wet' matt.note: Exactly — the chain reads only today's state. Nine wet days or one, rainy-today is rainy-today: 7/10.

Higher — wet weather feeds on itself.note: Tempting, but the chain has no streak counter. Each step reads only the current state, so the run doesn't compound — it stays 7/10.

hints: No wrong guess yet — commit and we'll test it on the chain.
The streak isn't part of the state; only today is.
It stays 7/10 — the run doesn't move it.

beats.prompt: Read this just-in-time primer on the Markov property, then continue.

interaction.title: Markov = memoryless
interaction.body: A process is **Markov (memoryless)** when the next state depends only on **where you are now** — the *state* — not the path that got you there. Formally `P(Xₙ₊₁ | X₀…Xₙ) = P(Xₙ₊₁ | Xₙ)`: the whole past collapses into the single current state.

feedback.correct: State, memoryless, `P(next | now)` — keep those three words.
hints: 'Where you are now' is the state.
Memoryless: everything before the current state is discarded — not the current state itself.
P(Xₙ₊₁ | X₀…Xₙ) = P(Xₙ₊₁ | Xₙ).

beats.prompt: Tap the Rainy node, then its out-edge to Rainy. What is P(rain tomorrow | rain today)?

feedback.correct: Condition on **today**, so read today's **row**. Rainy-today is row 2 `[3/10, 7/10]`; the rain-tomorrow edge is the 7/10 self-loop. **7/10**.
hints: Start on the node you're given: 'rain today' → tap the Rainy node, then its out-edge to Rainy.
Condition on today, so read today's row. Rainy-today is the second row [3/10, 7/10]; the entry for rain-tomorrow is 7/10. Rows are 'where you are now,' columns are 'where you go.'
The Rainy → Rainy self-loop is 7/10.

beats.prompt: Press Step and watch the token hop — only the current row decides the next state.

hero.structuralReadout: Only the current row fires: from Rainy the next draw is 7/10 rainy, 3/10 clear — the streak never enters the draw.

feedback.correct: Watch the token hop: each step the next state is drawn **only** from the current row — Clear fires `[3/5, 2/5]`, Rainy fires `[3/10, 7/10]`. The path of hops so far never enters the draw.
hints: Press Step. The lit row is the only thing the next draw uses.
The history of hops doesn't bias the next one — only the node you're standing on.
Sit on Rainy and the next draw is 3/10 clear, 7/10 rainy — every time, regardless of the run.

beats.prompt: Reveal each lens — edge, row, and draw all point to the same law.

lenses.label: Diagram edge
lenses.body: The edge out of Rainy reads 7/10 — it leaves the node you're on, nothing earlier.

lenses.label: Matrix row
lenses.body: Conditioning on today picks one row; that row alone is the next-step distribution.

lenses.label: Simulation draw
lenses.body: Each hop samples the current node's row — the chain of past hops never enters the draw.

beats.interviewNote: Memorylessness — *the* defining property — is the first thing a quant interview probes about a chain. State it as `P(Xₙ₊₁ | X₀…Xₙ) = P(Xₙ₊₁ | Xₙ)`: the current state is a sufficient statistic for the future.

feedback.correct: Edge, row, and draw all read **only the current state** ⇒ `P(Xₙ₊₁ | X₀…Xₙ) = P(Xₙ₊₁ | Xₙ)`. That's the Markov property.
hints: Reveal each lens — edge, row, draw.
Memoryless ≠ independent. Today matters a lot — 7/10 after rain vs 2/5 after clear. What's discarded is everything *before* today, not today itself.
All three keep only 'now': P(Xₙ₊₁ | past) = P(Xₙ₊₁ | Xₙ).

beats.prompt: Match each story to Markov or Not Markov.

pairs.left: Tomorrow's weather uses the last 3 days
Your score = the running max so far
pairs.right: Markov — the max is the state

feedback.correct: Markov isn't 'no past' — it's 'no past **beyond the current state**.' Only-today is Markov; last-3-days isn't (on the raw state); the running max is Markov because the max already **is** the state.
hints: Ask: does the next step need anything other than the single current state?
Depending on the past is fine — depending on more than the current **state** is the problem. Fold the history you need into the state and it's Markov again.
Only-today → Markov; last-3-days → not Markov (raw state); running-max → Markov.

beats.prompt: One chain eventually forgets its start. The other locks into a state forever. Which claim is true of absorbing chains?

options: Both eventually forget where they started
The absorbing one never forgets — once stuck, it stays
Neither forgets — Markov means perfect memory

Both eventually forget where they starte.note: Half-right: the ergodic weather chain forgets its start (L7, Pⁿ→π). But a chain with an absorbing state never forgets — once it lands there it can't leave. Memoryless ≠ can't-get-stuck.

The absorbing one never forgets — once s.note: Right. A memoryless chain can still have a one-way trapdoor: an absorbing state (L4). 'Memoryless' constrains the next step, not whether the long run can lock in.

Neither forgets — Markov means perfect m.note: Markov is the opposite of perfect memory — the next step reads only the current state. Some chains forget their start entirely (L7); others lock into an absorbing state (L4).

hints: No wrong guess — this seeds L4 (getting stuck) and L7 (forgetting the start).
Memoryless constrains the next step, not whether the long run can lock in.
Absorbing → never leaves; ergodic → forgets the start.

beats.prompt: Transfer challenge: a two-day momentum rule breaks the Markov property on {Up, Down} — augment the state to fix it. Compute P(up tomorrow | state = UU).

interaction.scenario: 'Hot streak': a stock ticks **up** tomorrow with probability \(3/4\) if it rose on **both** today and yesterday, otherwise \(1/4\). On the raw state \(\{\text{Up}, \text{Down}\}\) this isn't Markov — \(P(\text{up tomorrow} \mid \text{up today})\) is \(3/4\) after UU but \(1/4\) after DU. Redefine the state as the ordered pair **(yesterday, today)** to get a clean 4-state Markov chain.

feedback.correct: On \(\{\text{Up}, \text{Down}\}\) the rule is ill-defined (\(3/4\) after UU, \(1/4\) after DU) \(\Rightarrow\) not Markov. Augment the state to **(yesterday, today)**: from **UU**, up tomorrow is \(\mathbf{3/4}\). Same process, clean 4-state chain.
hints: The rule needs *two* days of history, so a single day isn't enough state. What pair of days determines tomorrow?

Only on the raw \(\{\text{Up}, \text{Down}\}\) state is it non-Markov — \(P(\text{up} \mid \text{today up})\) is ambiguous (\(3/4\) after UU, \(1/4\) after DU). Redefine the state as the ordered pair (yesterday, today) and the *same* process is a clean 4-state Markov chain. Augmentation is the fix.
State UU means it rose both days, so the streak rule fires at \(3/4\). \(P(\text{up} \mid UU) = 3/4\).

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: a two-day rule breaks the Markov property on {Clear, Rainy} — but the right state space fixes it. Compute P(rain tomorrow | state = RR).

interaction.scenario: 'Sticky weather': it rains tomorrow with probability \(4/5\) if it rained **both** today and yesterday, otherwise \(1/5\). On the raw state \(\{\text{Clear}, \text{Rainy}\}\) this isn't Markov — \(P(\text{rain tomorrow} \mid \text{rainy today})\) is \(4/5\) after RR but \(1/5\) after CR. Redefine the state as the ordered pair **(yesterday, today)** to get a clean 4-state Markov chain.

feedback.correct: On \(\{\text{Clear}, \text{Rainy}\}\) the rule is ill-defined (\(4/5\) after RR, \(1/5\) after CR) \(\Rightarrow\) not Markov. Augment the state to **(yesterday, today)**: from **RR**, rain tomorrow is \(\mathbf{4/5}\). Same process, clean 4-state chain.
hints: The rule needs *two* days, so one day isn't enough state. What pair of days determines tomorrow?

Only on the raw \(\{\text{Clear}, \text{Rainy}\}\) state is it non-Markov — \(P(\text{rain} \mid \text{today rainy})\) is ambiguous (\(4/5\) after RR, \(1/5\) after CR). Redefine the state as the ordered pair (yesterday, today) and the *same* process is a clean 4-state Markov chain. Augmentation is the fix.
State RR means it rained both days, so the rule fires at \(4/5\). \(P(\text{rain} \mid RR) = 4/5\).

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap to review the core principle.

feedback.correct: Only **now** matters: a Markov process reads the current state and discards the path. If the next step needs more history, **fold that history into the state** — then it's Markov again.
hints: Nine wet days or one — rainy-today is rainy-today: 7/10. The streak isn't in the state.
Memoryless ≠ independent: today matters (7/10 vs 2/5); what's discarded is everything before today.
Next up: pack a whole chain into one transition matrix P — every row a 'where you are now'.
